title: Charmed Life - Chapter 17 (Fragment 4)
text:
Cat thought he had been meaning to say something about freezing fog in the Grampians, but he drifted away to sleep while Janet talked, feeling snug and cared for.

He woke on Sunday morning, quite the opposite: cold and quivering. This afternoon he was due to be turned into a frog or face a tiger—and a rather heavy strong tiger Will Suggins would make too, he thought. Beyond the tiger—if there was a beyond—lay the horrors of Monday without magic. Julia and Roger might help there, except that it would be no use when Mr. Baslam came on Wednesday and demanded twenty pounds Cat knew he could not get. Mr. Nostrum was no help. Mrs. Sharp was even less. The only hope seemed to be to take Janet and some dragons’ blood to the forbidden garden and try to get away.

Cat climbed out of bed to go and get some dragons’ blood from Mr. Saunders’ workshop. Euphemia came in with his breakfast on a tray, and he had to climb back into bed again. Euphemia was quite as kind as she had been last night. Cat felt bad. And when he had finished breakfast, Millie came. She scooped Cat off his pillows and hugged him.

“You poor silly darling! Thank goodness you’re all right. I was aching to come and see you last night, but someone had to stay with our poor guests. Now, you’re to stay in bed all today, and you must ask for anything you want. What would you like?”

“I couldn’t have some dragons’ blood, could I?” Cat asked hopefully.

Millie laughed. “Good heavens, Eric! You go and have that fearsome accident and then you ask for the most dangerous stuff in the world. No, you may not have dragons’ blood. It’s one of the few things in the Castle that really are forbidden.”

“Like Chrestomanci’s garden?” Cat asked.

“ Not quite like that,” Millie answered. “The garden is old as the hills and stuffed with magic of every kind. That’s dangerous in another way. Everything’s stronger there. You’ll be taken into the garden when you know enough magic to understand it. But dragons’ blood is so harmful that I’m never happy even when Michael uses it. You’re on no account to touch any.”

Julia and Roger came in next, dressed ready for church, with armfuls of books and toys and a great many interested questions. They were so kind that Cat was quite unhappy by the time Janet arrived. He did not want to leave the Castle. He felt he was truly settling in to it.

“That lump of dough is still stuck to your carpet,” Janet said gloomily, which made Cat feel rather less settled. “I’ve just been seeing Chrestomanci, and it is hard to be punished for other people’s sins,” Janet went on, “even though I’ve been rewarded with the sight of a sky-blue dressing gown with golden lions on it.”

“I’ve not seen that one,” said Cat.

“I think he has one for every day of the week,” said Janet. “All he needed was a flaming sword. He forbade me to go to church. The vicar won’t have me because of what Gwendolen did last Sunday. And I was so cross at being blamed for it that I’d got my mouth open to say I wasn’t Gwendolen when I remembered that if I went to church I’d have to wear that stupid white hat with little holes in it—can he hear through that mirror, do you think?”

vocabulary.definition: (in the phrase 'on no account') for no reason, definitely not.
vocabulary.example: You’re on no account to touch any.

vocabulary.definition: At or to the further side of.
vocabulary.example: Beyond the tiger—if there was a beyond—lay the horrors of Monday without magic.

vocabulary.definition: To say or think that someone is responsible for a fault or wrong.
vocabulary.example: I was so cross at being blamed for it.

vocabulary.definition: Annoyed or angry.
vocabulary.example: I was so cross at being blamed for it.

vocabulary.definition: Able or likely to cause harm or injury.
vocabulary.example: You ask for the most dangerous stuff in the world.

vocabulary.definition: To ask for urgently or forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Mr. Baslam came on Wednesday and demanded twenty pounds.

vocabulary.definition: Wearing clothes of a particular type or style.
vocabulary.example: Julia and Roger came in next, dressed ready for church.

vocabulary.definition: To be carried slowly by a current of air or water; to move aimlessly.
vocabulary.example: ...but he drifted away to sleep while Janet talked.

vocabulary.definition: Scheduled or expected to happen at a particular time.
vocabulary.example: This afternoon he was due to be turned into a frog or face a tiger.

vocabulary.definition: A character's name in the text.
vocabulary.example: Euphemia came in with his breakfast on a tray.

vocabulary.definition: Causing fear; frightening.
vocabulary.example: You go and have that fearsome accident.

vocabulary.definition: Burning with a bright flame.
vocabulary.example: All he needed was a flaming sword.

vocabulary.definition: To order someone not to do something.
vocabulary.example: He forbade me to go to church.

vocabulary.definition: Not allowed; prohibited.
vocabulary.example: ...take Janet and some dragons’ blood to the forbidden garden.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely cold, causing freezing or frost.
vocabulary.example: Cat thought he had been meaning to say something about freezing fog in the Grampians.

vocabulary.definition: In a sad or depressed manner.
vocabulary.example: Janet said gloomily.

vocabulary.definition: Causing or capable of causing harm.
vocabulary.example: Dragons’ blood is so harmful that I’m never happy even when Michael uses it.

vocabulary.definition: Things that cause feelings of fear or disgust.
vocabulary.example: ...lay the horrors of Monday without magic.

vocabulary.definition: Held (someone) tightly in one's arms to show affection.
vocabulary.example: She scooped Cat off his pillows and hugged him.

vocabulary.definition: The feeling of wanting to learn or know about something.
vocabulary.example: ...with armfuls of books and toys and a great many interested questions.

vocabulary.definition: A compact mass of a substance without a definite shape.
vocabulary.example: That lump of dough is still stuck to your carpet.

vocabulary.definition: To cause suffering due to wrongdoing.
vocabulary.example: It is hard to be punished for other people’s sins.

vocabulary.definition: Shaking slightly, typically through nervousness or cold.
vocabulary.example: He woke...cold and quivering.

vocabulary.definition: Given a benefit in recognition of service or achievement.
vocabulary.example: ...even though I’ve been rewarded with the sight of a sky-blue dressing gown.

vocabulary.definition: Becoming comfortable in a new environment.
vocabulary.example: He felt he was truly settling in to it.

vocabulary.definition: Comfortably warm and protected.
vocabulary.example: ...feeling snug and cared for.

vocabulary.definition: Filled tightly with something.
vocabulary.example: The garden is old as the hills and stuffed with magic of every kind.

vocabulary.definition: To grasp the meaning or significance of.
vocabulary.example: You’ll be taken into the garden when you know enough magic to understand it.

vocabulary.definition: A room or building where things are made or repaired.
vocabulary.example: Cat climbed out of bed to get some dragons’ blood from Mr. Saunders’ workshop.
